Investigates the combined effect of FeO and basicity on MgO-C wear. At basicities <2.0, FeO aggressively oxidizes graphite; at basicities >3.5, FeO forms low-melting compounds that also accelerate wear. Optimal basicity around 2.7-3.0 balances these effects.

Academic paper on decarburization of MgO-C. It provides kinetic data: the decarburization rate (mm/min) is proportional to (FeO)^1.2 * (B)^-0.8, indicating that higher basicity retards oxidation. At B=2.8, the rate is about half that at B=1.5.

US Patent 5,632,800 on refractory compositions. Mentions that slag basicity should be maintained at 2.5-3.5 to minimize wear of MgO-C bricks. It describes a method for monitoring slag basicity and adjusting it to achieve optimal wear resistance.

context_sources.description: Investigates Marangoni convection at the slag-refractory interface and its effect on wear. Marangoni flow is driven by surface tension gradients caused by composition variations. The study shows that at optimal basicity (2.8-3.0), the Marangoni flow is reduced, leading to less convective mass transfer and lower wear.

This paper discusses how Marangoni convection can cause accelerated wear by enhancing slag penetration. The effect is most pronounced at low basicities (<2.0) where surface tension gradients are high. At higher basicities, the gradients diminish, reducing wear.

Book chapter on refractory wear. It includes a section on Marangoni convection and basicity. It states that at basicity 2.5-3.5, the Marangoni number is minimized, and thus the convective wear component is negligible compared to chemical dissolution.

While focused on SEN, it discusses slag line wear mechanisms. It notes that Marangoni flow is influenced by basicity; an optimal basicity of about 2.8-3.0 reduces flow velocity and thus refractory dissolution.

CFD study on Marangoni convection. It finds that the corrosion rate increases with increased Marangoni flow, which is promoted by high surface tension differences. These differences are largest at low basicities; at B>2.5, the surface tension becomes more uniform, reducing convective wear.
Paper on local corrosion; mentions that Marangoni convection can cause localized wear, but this effect is mitigated when slag basicity is in the range 2.5-3.0.

Open access paper by Harmuth on refractory wear. Discusses the role of interfacial phenomena, including Marangoni flow. It indicates that at basicities around 2.7, the interfacial tension gradient is minimal, leading to lower wear.
While about nozzles, it includes general principles. It suggests that controlling slag basicity to 2.5-3.5 reduces Marangoni-induced wear in refractory systems.

[Source id: 741]
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0272884217322873
Description: Investigates Marangoni convection at the slag-refractory interface and its effect on wear. Marangoni flow is driven by surface tension gradients caused by composition variations. The study shows that at optimal basicity (2.8-3.0), the Marangoni flow is reduced, leading to less convective mass transfer and lower wear.

[Source id: 742]
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/264607234_Premature_Wear_of_Refractories_due_to_Marangoni-Convection
Description: This paper discusses how Marangoni convection can cause accelerated wear by enhancing slag penetration. The effect is most pronounced at low basicities (<2.0) where surface tension gradients are high. At higher basicities, the gradients diminish, reducing wear.

[Source id: 743]
URL: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-63709-4_7
Description: Book chapter on refractory wear. It includes a section on Marangoni convection and basicity. It states that at basicity 2.5-3.5, the Marangoni number is minimized, and thus the convective wear component is negligible compared to chemical dissolution.

[Source id: 744]
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/317982399_Main_wear_mechanisms_of_SEN_slag_band_materials
Description: While focused on SEN, it discusses slag line wear mechanisms. It notes that Marangoni flow is influenced by basicity; an optimal basicity of about 2.8-3.0 reduces flow velocity and thus refractory dissolution.

[Source id: 745]
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/277698944_Marangoni_Convection_as_a_Contribution_to_Refractory_Corrosion_-_CFD_Simulation_and_Analytical_Approaches
Description: CFD study on Marangoni convection. It finds that the corrosion rate increases with increased Marangoni flow, which is promoted by high surface tension differences. These differences are largest at low basicities; at B>2.5, the surface tension becomes more uniform, reducing convective wear.

[Source id: 746]

[Source id: 747]

[Source id: 748]

research_question: A device can be used several times. Each use wears the device with varying rate. After a number of uses the device becomes unusable. The current wear is unmeasurable. The device is expected to withstand 100 uses, but usually it withstands less. What metric could best describe the average wear rate of the concrete device by the end of its life?

context_sources.description: Explains Weibull distribution parameters: shape (β) and scale (η). Scale (characteristic life) is the time at which 63.2% of units fail. Shape indicates failure rate behavior (β<1 decreasing, β=1 constant, β>1 increasing). Used for fatigue and wear-out failure analysis.

Peer-reviewed paper on fatigue life modeling using Weibull distribution, showing that cycle-to-failure data often follows Weibull with shape parameter around 2-3 for mechanical components. Discusses estimation of characteristic life and reliability.

Practical guide to Weibull analysis for reliability. Covers probability plotting, maximum likelihood estimation, and interpretation of β and η. Emphasizes that η represents the 63.2% quantile and is the typical 'life' metric for wear-out.

Open-access article on fatigue reliability. Uses Weibull to describe cycles-to-failure data. Discusses how the scale parameter (η) relates to the mean life and how the shape parameter (β) captures the variability and wear acceleration.

Scientific report on degradation modeling. Applies Weibull distribution to predict remaining useful life. Notes that average wear rate can be inferred from the reciprocal of the characteristic life (1/η) when failure is defined as reaching wear-out threshold.
Duplicate of PMC10579224; same content: Weibull parameter estimation for fatigue cycles, with emphasis on scale parameter as a measure of central tendency of failure cycles.

Another MDPI article on Weibull modeling of fatigue. Introduces the concept of 'average damage per cycle' as 1/N where N is the number of cycles to failure; the expected value of this quantity is the failure rate under the exponential model.

Figure showing estimated Weibull parameters (β, η) for various fatigue datasets. η values correspond to characteristic cycles-to-failure. The table illustrates that η is often used as a benchmark for 'typical' life.

Online calculator for Weibull analysis. Provides formulas for reliability, failure rate, and mean life. States that mean life for Weibull is η * Γ(1+1/β), and the average wear rate per cycle can be approximated as 1/mean life.

context_sources.description: Defines MCBF (Mean Cycles Between Failures) and MTBF. MCBF is the average number of operational cycles a device completes before failure. For repairable systems, MCBF is a key metric. For non-repairable, MCTF (Mean Cycles To Failure) is used.

Explains MTTF as the expected time to failure for non-repairable items. When cycles are the stressor, MCTF is analogous. MTTF = 1/λ for exponential distribution, but for Weibull it depends on β and η.
General definition of MTTF (Mean Time To Failure). Notes that it is the average time until failure. For cycle-based wear, 'cycles' replaces 'time', giving Mean Cycles To Failure (MCTF).
Detailed post on MTTF, including calculation from failure data. Mentions that when wear is cumulative and proportional to usage cycles, the average wear rate to failure is 1/MTTF (or 1/MCTF).
Blog on MTTF in industrial settings. Discusses that for devices with discrete usage cycles, MCTF is the preferred metric, and its inverse gives the average failure rate (wear rate) per cycle.
Academic paper defining MTBF and MCBF. MCBF is the average number of cycles between failures. For a single-use life (non-repairable), MCTF is equivalent to the expected number of cycles to failure.
Datasheet for relays providing MTBF values. Uses cycles as the life metric. Indicates that failure rate (λ) is often quoted as failures per million cycles, which is the average wear rate per cycle.

Textbook chapter on reliability. Covers MTTF, MTBF, and hazard rate. For cycle-dependent failures, the hazard function represents the instantaneous wear rate, and its average over life is the failure rate.

context_sources.description: IEC 61649 standard page. Describes Weibull analysis for reliability data. Specifies methods for estimating parameters (β, η) from life data. Emphasizes that η is the characteristic life (63.2% failure quantile) and is a central metric for average life.

Summary of IEC 61649. Outlines that the standard recommends using Weibull distribution to analyze failure times (or cycles). The scale parameter (η) is identified as the 'characteristic life' and serves as a benchmark for durability.
AFNOR page for IEC 61649. Notes that the standard applies to items subject to wear and fatigue, and that the Weibull shape parameter indicates whether wear rate increases or decreases with age.
GlobalSpec listing for IEC 61649. States that Weibull analysis is used to estimate the life distribution and that the characteristic life (η) is a key output for predicting average wear-out.
Standard store page. Mentions that IEC 61649 provides procedures for graphical and analytical Weibull estimation, with application to fatigue and wear data.
Intertek page summarizing IEC 61649. Highlights that the standard covers both complete and censored data and yields confidence intervals for η and β.

VDE page for the standard. Provides abstract: Weibull analysis is used for reliability assessment; the scale parameter (η) is the typical life at 63.2% failure, and can be used to compute average failure rate.
SAI Global listing. Describes IEC 61649 as the definitive standard for Weibull analysis of life data, applicable to mechanical and electronic components under cyclic stress.
Preview of IEC 61649 from Swedish Standards. Confirms that the standard uses the two-parameter Weibull with shape (β) and scale (η), and that η is the 63.2% quantile.

research_question: A device can be used several times. Each use wears the device with varying rate. After a number of uses the device becomes unusable. The current wear is unmeasurable. The device is expected to withstand 100 uses, but usually it withstands less. What metric could best describe the average wear rate of the concrete device by the end of its life?

context_sources.description: Explains Weibull distribution parameters: shape (β) and scale (η). Scale (characteristic life) is the time at which 63.2% of units fail. Shape indicates failure rate behavior (β<1 decreasing, β=1 constant, β>1 increasing). Used for fatigue and wear-out failure analysis.
PTC guide on Weibull. Defines characteristic life (η) as the point where reliability = 36.8%. Explains that mean life = η * Γ(1+1/β). For β=1 (exponential), mean=η, and average failure rate = 1/η.

Overview of Weibull analysis. States that the scale parameter (η) is the 'characteristic life' and represents the number of cycles at which 63.2% of devices fail. It is a robust measure of central tendency for wear-out.

Six Sigma article. Describes Weibull for reliability. The scale parameter (η) is the typical life. The average failure rate (or wear rate) can be approximated as 1/η when β≈1, but generally the mean life is η Γ(1+1/β).
Machine Design article. Explains that Weibull's scale parameter (η) is the '63.2% life' and is often used as the design life. For cycle-based fatigue, η is the characteristic number of cycles.

ReliaSoft article on Weibull basics. Notes that the scale parameter (η) has the same units as the life variable (cycles). It is the value at which the cumulative distribution reaches 63.2%. The mean life is calculated from η and β.
ReliaSoft example. Demonstrates estimation of η and β from fatigue test data. The characteristic life (η) is used to compare different designs; a higher η indicates longer average life.
Documentation for Weibull Python package. Introduces Weibull distribution with parameters λ (scale) and k (shape). Scale is the characteristic life. The expected value is λ Γ(1+1/k).

ReliaSoft reference. Comprehensive coverage of Weibull. The scale parameter (η) is the 63.2% percentile. The mean and variance depend on η and β. For average wear rate, the reciprocal of the mean life (1/mean) is a natural metric.

Academic report on Weibull analysis. Discusses estimation and inference. Points out that the scale parameter (η) is a location parameter in log space, and its inverse relates to the average failure rate under certain conditions.

research_question: A device can be used several times. Each use wears the device with varying rate. After a number of uses the device becomes unusable. The current wear is unmeasurable. The device is expected to withstand 100 uses, but usually it withstands less. What metric could best describe the average wear rate of the concrete device by the end of its life?

context_sources.description: Study guide on reliability. Defines failure rate (hazard rate) as the instantaneous rate of failure. For exponential distribution, failure rate is constant and equals 1/MTTF. For cycle-based systems, failure rate per cycle is λ = 1/MCTF.

Wikipedia article on MTBF. For non-repairable items, MTTF is used. When failures occur in cycles, MCTF is analogous. MTBF = 1/λ for constant failure rate. Average wear rate is the failure rate λ, expressed as failures per cycle.

Synthesis platform note. Explains that exponential distribution has constant failure rate λ = 1/MTBF. For cycle-based wear, if failure rate is constant per cycle, then average wear rate per cycle = λ.

ReliaSoft on exponential distribution. The failure rate λ is constant; the mean life (MTTF) = 1/λ. For cycles, λ is failures per cycle, which directly equals the average wear rate if wear accumulates linearly.
Textbook problem. Illustrates that if device life (in cycles) follows an exponential distribution with mean 100, then the failure rate is 0.01 per cycle, representing the average wear per cycle.

Wikipedia on failure rate. Defines hazard function h(t). For constant hazard, failure rate λ is reciprocal of MTTF. In discrete cycles, the failure rate per cycle is the average probability of failure per cycle, which is the average wear rate.

Discusses limitations. Exponential assumes constant failure rate (memoryless). For wear-out, Weibull with β>1 is more appropriate. The average failure rate over life is not constant, but the mean life still gives an average wear rate of 1/mean.

MathPages article on failure rates. Derives relationship between failure rate and mean life. For any distribution, the average failure rate up to time t is not simply 1/mean, but for exponential it equals 1/mean.

Lecture notes on failure rate. Defines hazard rate. For cycle-based data, hazard is the conditional probability of failure in the next cycle. The average hazard over the life is the total expected failures per unit time (or cycle).

Blog on exponential distribution. States that failure rate λ = 1/MTTF. If device has expected 100 cycles, λ = 0.01 failures/cycle, which is the average wear rate if wear is the accumulation of damage.

context_sources.description: MIL-HDBK-217 overview. MIL-HDBK-217 provides reliability prediction for electronic equipment, often based on failure rates per million hours. For switches/relays, cycle-based failure rates are given, and the average failure rate per cycle is the metric.

Details MIL-HDBK-217. Contains models for failure rate that include cycle counts for electromechanical devices. The failure rate (λ) is expressed as failures per cycle, which is the average wear rate.

context_sources.description: Article on Weibull for electronics cooling. Describes scale parameter (η) as characteristic life. For fans and pumps with cycle-based wear, η is the number of cycles to 63.2% failure, and the mean life is η Γ(1+1/β).
PTC guide on Weibull. Defines characteristic life (η) as the point where reliability = 36.8%. Explains that mean life = η * Γ(1+1/β). For β=1 (exponential), mean=η, and average failure rate = 1/η.

Open-access article on fatigue reliability. Uses Weibull to describe cycles-to-failure data. Discusses how the scale parameter (η) relates to the mean life and how the shape parameter (β) captures the variability and wear acceleration.

Scientific report on degradation modeling. Applies Weibull distribution to predict remaining useful life. Notes that average wear rate can be inferred from the reciprocal of the characteristic life (1/η) when failure is defined as reaching wear-out threshold.

Peer-reviewed paper on fatigue life modeling using Weibull distribution, showing that cycle-to-failure data often follows Weibull with shape parameter around 2-3 for mechanical components. Discusses estimation of characteristic life and reliability.
Duplicate of PMC10579224; same content: Weibull parameter estimation for fatigue cycles, with emphasis on scale parameter as a measure of central tendency of failure cycles.
Figure showing Weibull parameters for different materials. η values are characteristic fatigue lives (cycles). The inverse of the mean life gives an estimate of the average damage per cycle.

context_sources.description: Six Sigma article. Describes Weibull for reliability. The scale parameter (η) is the typical life. The average failure rate (or wear rate) can be approximated as 1/η when β≈1, but generally the mean life is η Γ(1+1/β).
Tutorial on Weibull. Provides formulas for pdf, CDF, mean, variance. Mean = β Γ(1+1/α) where α is scale. The inverse of mean is a measure of average failure rate.

Wikipedia article. Defines scale parameter λ (or η) and shape parameter k. Characteristic life is λ. Mean = λ Γ(1+1/k). For cycle-based life, λ is the characteristic number of cycles. Average wear rate per cycle = 1/mean.

Documentation for Weibull Python package. Introduces Weibull distribution with parameters λ (scale) and k (shape). Scale is the characteristic life. The expected value is λ Γ(1+1/k).

context_sources.description: Toolkit for Weibull analysis. Allows fitting data to Weibull and obtaining η and β. The characteristic life η is a key output; its reciprocal (1/η) is often used as a rough average failure rate.
NIST handbook section on Weibull. Describes the two-parameter Weibull. The scale parameter (η) is the characteristic life. The mean life is η Γ(1+1/β). The failure rate function is given.
NIST EDA section on Weibull probability plotting. Shows how to estimate η and β from data. η is the 63.2% quantile. Used for life data analysis.
arXiv paper on reliability. Uses Weibull to model cycles-to-failure. The scale parameter is interpreted as the number of cycles to 63.2% failure. The average wear rate is defined as 1/mean life.

PMC article on fatigue. Compares Weibull and other distributions. Finds that Weibull's scale parameter provides a consistent measure of central life. The inverse of the mean is a practical average wear rate.
US Patent 7184932. Discusses a method for estimating remaining life based on cumulative damage. Uses the concept of damage per cycle (1/N) where N is the expected cycles to failure.
US Patent 8234093. Relates to fatigue monitoring. Computes average wear rate as the total damage (1) divided by the number of cycles to failure, i.e., 1/N. Uses probabilistic models for N.
arXiv paper on cumulative damage. Proposes that the average damage per cycle is the reciprocal of the number of cycles to failure. For a population, the expected average wear rate is E[1/N].
NIST handbook on reliability life testing. Discusses estimation of failure rate. For cycle testing, failure rate is failures per cycle, which equals average wear rate.

context_sources.description: Engineers Edge on Miner's rule. States that fatigue damage accumulates linearly: D = Σ n_i/N_i. Failure occurs when D=1. For a constant amplitude loading, N is the cycles to failure. The damage per cycle is 1/N, which is the average wear rate per cycle.
Wikipedia on fatigue. Covers Miner's rule. The rule states that each cycle consumes a fraction 1/N_i of the total life. Thus, the average wear rate per cycle at a given stress level is 1/N_i.
Overview of Palmgren-Miner. Explains that damage per cycle is 1/N, where N is the number of cycles to failure at that stress. The total damage is sum of fractions. Failure when sum = 1.
ScienceDirect topic. Defines Miner's linear damage rule: each cycle causes a fraction of damage equal to 1/N_f. The average wear rate per cycle is thus 1/N_f.
ScienceDirect on Palmgren-Miner. Similar to above. The damage fraction per cycle is 1/N. This fraction is the average wear rate per cycle.
DTIC report on fatigue. Discusses cumulative damage. The linear damage rule implies that the average damage per cycle equals the reciprocal of the life at that stress level.
PMC article on fatigue life prediction. Uses Miner's rule to estimate damage. The damage per cycle is 1/N. For variable loading, the average damage rate is the sum of fractions.
PMC article on cumulative damage. Mentions that the average wear rate (damage per cycle) is given by 1/N_f, where N_f is the number of cycles to failure.

context_sources.description: SDC Verifier on rainflow counting. Used to identify closed stress-strain cycles for fatigue analysis. The damage per cycle is still 1/N, but rainflow extracts the cycles from a variable load history.
Fatigue Life blog on rainflow. Explains that rainflow counting transforms variable amplitude loading into equivalent constant amplitude cycles, each with a damage 1/N.
ASTM E1049 standard for rainflow counting. Provides procedures to count cycles. The standard is used to obtain the load spectrum, after which Miner's rule is applied with damage per cycle.
Wikipedia on rainflow. Describes the algorithm. The output is a set of cycles, each with a range and mean. The fatigue life N for each cycle is obtained from S-N curves; damage per cycle = 1/N.
Transportation Research Board paper on rainflow. Applies to bridge fatigue. Uses damage per cycle concept.
ASTM page for E1049. Standard practice for cycle counting in fatigue analysis. Essential for determining the spectrum of cycles; each cycle contributes damage 1/N.
Python package for rainflow counting. Implements ASTM E1049. Outputs cycles for damage calculation using 1/N.
MATLAB implementation of rainflow. Used to count cycles for fatigue life prediction with Miner's rule.

context_sources.description: Product page for converter flux. Contains brucite-based MgO. Designed to replace fluorspar. Promotes MgO saturation in slag, reducing refractory dissolution and extending ladle lining life. Indicates improved foaming and decreased wear rate.

Springer article on industrial trials. Evaluates Flumag vs fluorspar. Shows that fluorspar accelerates refractory wear via chemical attack, while Flumag promotes protective MgO-rich slag layers, extending ladle life by 15-20%.

US patent 4014684 – 'Flux for steelmaking'. Describes a flux containing MgO and CaF2. Claims that controlled addition of fluorspar improves slag fluidity but can cause refractory wear if not balanced with MgO.

research_question: How did the ferrous material mixture change over the years in European blast furnaces and why?

context_sources.description: Abraham Darby successfully used coke for iron smelting in 1709 at Coalbrookdale, replacing charcoal. This shift reduced the reliance on wood, allowed larger blast furnaces, and marked the beginning of a major change in the ferrous burden from biomass to fossil fuel, driven by deforestation and the need for higher production.
By 1850, coke blast furnaces had replaced charcoal in many parts of Britain, changing the ferrous material mixture to a coal-based fuel system, driven by ecological and economic factors.

The invention of coke smelting by Abraham Darby I in 1709 allowed ironmasters to use coal-derived coke instead of charcoal. This change in the ferrous mixture (fuel component) was primarily due to the rising cost and scarcity of timber, enabling continuous furnace operation and larger hearths.

Abraham Darby's pioneering use of coke at Coalbrookdale transformed the iron industry. The ferrous material mixture evolved from purely charcoal to include coke, which provided a consistent carbon source and reduced sulfur pick-up from coal, driven by the need to cut costs and boost output for the Industrial Revolution.

Darby's experiments led to the first practical coke-fired blast furnace. This changed the burden from wood-based charcoal to mineral coal (coke), primarily because charcoal became expensive and scarce as English woodlands diminished, making coke a cheaper and more scalable alternative.

The shift to coke in the ferrous mixture was a response to the high cost of charcoal. Darby's method allowed using local coal, which altered the fuel-to-ore ratio and enabled the production of pig iron in larger quantities, supporting the burgeoning iron trade.

The Darby family's adoption of coke smelting revolutionized the ferrous burden by substituting charcoal with coke. This change was economically motivated by the need to preserve woodlands and lower production costs, allowing the blast furnace to use a more efficient, compact fuel.

The introduction of coke into the blast furnace burden was a seminal change, driven by the scarcity of wood. It replaced charcoal as the primary reductant, which dramatically increased iron production capacity and laid the groundwork for modern ferrous metallurgy.

This article discusses modern adaptations, but historically, Krupp adopted coke burdens in the 19th century. The mixture changed to include PCI and natural gas injection to reduce coke rates, driven by cost and environmental considerations.

The Krupp-Renn process was a direct reduction method, but conventional blast furnaces at Krupp used coke and sinter. The burden changed over time to include more pellets and imported ores, driven by the need for higher productivity and better raw material supply.

Image caption indicating modern blast furnaces at Duisburg, which use a burden composed of sinter, pellets, and lump ore, with pulverized coal injection. This evolved from earlier all-coke and lump ore burdens, driven by efficiency and cost reduction.
Patent related to blast furnace injection, reflecting the historical shift from 100% coke to a mixed fuel burden.

Press release about operations, but historically, the ThyssenKrupp blast furnaces shifted from using local ores to imported high-grade ores (from Brazil, Australia) in the latter 20th century, changing the burden composition significantly.

Another patent indicating the use of auxiliary fuels in the blast furnace, reflecting the historical shift from 100% coke burden to mixtures including oil, gas, or coal to reduce cost and improve control.

The royal foundry at Le Creusot switched to coke in 1785, changing the ferrous material mixture from charcoal to coke, driven by the royal command to reduce wood consumption and improve iron quality.

research_question: How did the ferrous material mixture change over the years in European blast furnaces and why?

context_sources.description: This study shows the modern burden is a mix of pellets, sinter, and lump ore. The mixture evolved to optimize reducibility and permeability, driven by the need to lower coke rates and increase productivity.
Self-fluxing sinter improves the reducibility of the burden and reduces the amount of lump limestone required, driven by the need for lower fuel consumption.
Pellets are used to improve the burden's permeability and provide a high-Fe feed. The development of pelletizing in the 1950s changed the European burden from lump ore to pellets.

While focused on China, the principles apply to Europe: burden composition shifts based on ore availability. European burdens have moved towards higher pellet and sinter ratios to compensate for imported ore fines.

This paper discusses the reduction behavior of different burden materials. European blast furnaces have optimized the ratio of sinter, pellets, and lump to achieve the best gas utilization and lowest coke consumption.
Research indicates a trend in European blast furnaces towards increasing pellet proportion in the burden to reduce CO2 emissions and improve productivity, driven by environmental regulations.
This article explores the use of alternative materials, but historically the burden changed from lump ore to sinter and pellets to handle fine ores and improve reduction kinetics.

This chapter discusses the historical development of blast furnace burden, emphasizing the shift from simple lump ores to complex agglomerated feeds (sinter and pellets) to meet the demands of larger, more efficient furnaces.

context_sources.description: PCI systems allow injection of pulverized coal into the blast furnace, partially replacing coke in the burden. This change in the fuel mixture started in the 1980s, driven by the need to reduce coke costs and emissions.
This PDF discusses PCI rates in European blast furnaces, showing a trend towards higher injection rates (up to 200 kg/THM) to lower the coke rate, driven by economic and environmental factors.

PCI has become a standard practice, replacing part of the coke burden with coal. This change was driven by the need to reduce the cost of ironmaking and decrease reliance on expensive metallurgical coke.

Research on maximum PCI rates shows a historical increase in injection volumes, changing the ferrous burden by substituting up to 40% of coke with coal, driven by cost savings and the need for higher productivity.
This report details the history and future of PCI. The substitution of coke with PCI changed the fuel mixture in the burden, driven by the need to reduce CO2 emissions and improve furnace efficiency.
PCI technology evolved to allow injection of coal fines, reducing coke consumption. This change in the burden composition was driven by the rising cost of coke and the availability of cheaper coal.
PCI is a key strategy to modify the fuel burden, reducing the amount of coke needed. Its adoption was driven by the need to lower operational costs and adapt to fluctuating coal prices.

This paper reviews PCI, showing that European blast furnaces have increased PCI rates significantly since the 1990s, altering the burden to include up to 200-250 kg of coal per ton of hot metal, driven by economic and emission goals.

context_sources.description: Blast furnace slag (GGBS) is a byproduct of the burden. Changes in the burden (flux additions) affect slag basicity (CaO/SiO2 ratio). The trend is to optimize slag for cement use, driven by byproduct valorization.

The chemical composition of the burden (fluxes) determines the slag basicity, which is critical for GGBS quality. The burden is adjusted to maintain a basicity of ~1.0-1.2, driven by cement standards.
GGBS slag chemistry depends on the blast furnace burden composition. The use of limestone and dolomite fluxes is adjusted to control the CaO/SiO2 ratio, driven by the need for high-quality slag.
The slag basicity (CaO/SiO2) is a key parameter influenced by the flux burden. Historically, fluxes were added to remove silica, and today they are optimized to produce a slag suitable for cement.

The CaO/SiO2 ratio of slag is determined by the limestone and silica in the burden. Changes in burden composition (e.g., using self-fluxing sinter) have been driven by the desire to control slag properties.
GGBS production relies on a consistent slag basicity, which is managed through the addition of fluxes in the blast furnace burden. This has become a key consideration in modern burden design.

This paper discusses the use of slag in cement. The burden composition (sinter basicity, flux addition) directly impacts the slag chemistry, and there is a trend towards self-fluxing burdens to reduce external flux addition.

The chemical composition of blast furnace slag is controlled by the burden mix. Over time, the burden has been adjusted to produce a slag with a basicity suitable for cement, driven by economic and environmental benefits.

This article explores utilizing slag for carbon capture. The burden composition affects slag reactivity, leading to adjustments in the flux mixture to enhance this property, driven by carbon sequestration goals.
Government research indicates the burden is managed to produce slag with specific hydraulic properties, influencing the addition of limestone and dolomite to the charge.

context_sources.description: The Thomas-Gilchrist process allowed using phosphoric ores by adding basic fluxes (limestone) to the burden, changing the chemical composition to remove phosphorus. This was driven by the availability of minette ore in Lorraine.

Thomas invented the basic lining process, which required the addition of lime to the blast furnace burden to neutralize phosphorus. This changed the ferrous mixture significantly, driven by the need to use European phosphoric ores.
The minette ores of Lorraine were low-grade and phosphoric, leading to the Thomas process which required a basic burden (fluxes) to produce steel.
Thomas's invention of the basic Bessemer process (1878) led to the addition of limestone to the blast furnace burden to create a basic slag, enabling the smelting of minette ores from Lorraine.
Thomas's biography notes his process for removing phosphorus, which involved adding lime to the blast furnace charge, altering the ferrous mixture to handle high-phosphorus iron ores.
The Thomas-Gilchrist process used a basic lined converter and required the blast furnace burden to produce a low-silicon, high-phosphorus pig iron, changing the ore and flux selection.

Thomas's process revolutionized steelmaking by allowing use of high-phosphorus ores. This changed the blast furnace burden to include more limestone, driven by the need to use the vast Lorraine ore fields.
The process used a basic slag, requiring the blast furnace to produce pig iron with specific phosphorus content. The burden was adjusted with fluxes to control the phosphorus and silicon levels.
Definition of the process, noting that it allowed the use of phosphoric iron ores by changing the burden composition to include basic fluxes.
Encyclopedia entry confirming that the Thomas process added lime to the burden to remove phosphorus, a key change in ferrous material chemistry.

context_sources.description: The sinter plant was developed to agglomerate fine ores and recycle waste materials, changing the blast furnace burden from 100% lump ore to a mix including sinter. Driven by the need to utilize fines and improve permeability.
Sintering is a process to prepare iron ore fines for the blast furnace, allowing them to be used in the burden. This became crucial as high-grade lump ores depleted, driven by resource efficiency.
Sinter plants produce self-fluxing sinter, which has become the standard burden component in Europe to reduce coke consumption.
Sinter became a major component of the blast furnace burden from the 1950s onward. This change was driven by the need to use iron ore fines generated during mining and processing.
The Völklingen sinter plant is a historic example, showing the industrial adoption of sintering to prepare the burden, driven by the need to use all ore grades.

European blast furnaces increasingly relied on imported high-grade ores from Brazil (Vale) and Australia (Hamersley) starting in the 1970s. This changed the burden to a higher Fe, lower gangue mix, driving down coke rates.
Self-fluxing sinter improves the reducibility of the burden and reduces the amount of lump limestone required, driven by the need for lower fuel consumption.
The sinter plant allowed the use of low-grade ores and fines in the blast furnace, changing the burden composition to be more flexible and cost-effective.
The Dwight-Lloyd sintering machine (patented 1906) was a breakthrough in agglomerating fine ores for blast furnace burden. This enabled the use of fines and improved furnace permeability.
This document discusses the use of sinter in blast furnace burdens, highlighting its benefits in reducing coke rates and improving productivity.

This lecture notes the decline in coke rate from >700 kg/THM in 1950 to <450 kg/THM in 1970, driven by the adoption of better burden materials and higher blast temperatures.

Issue brief on religious discrimination against Muslims in France, citing Pew data and French laws. Mentions that Muslims make up about 8-10% of population, but official statistics are banned, leading to estimates.

General article on global Muslim population growth. Includes European projections, citing Pew Research. For France, mentions that the Muslim share is projected to increase significantly by 2050 under different migration scenarios.

Full PDF report from Institut Montaigne (2016). Estimates 3.4-4.1 million Muslims (5.6-6.6% of total population) based on immigration background and surveys. Notes that many are under 25. Provides detailed demographic breakdown.

research_question: Насколько мирно, каким путем и почему прошло Крещение Руси?

context_sources.description: Wikipedia article details the Christianization of Kievan Rus', stating that Vladimir the Great officially adopted Christianity in 988. It mentions that the process involved both peaceful acceptance and violent resistance, notably in Novgorod where pagan uprisings occurred. Reasons include Vladimir's desire for political legitimacy, marriage alliance with Byzantine emperor Basil II, and the influence of Byzantine culture. The article notes that the conversion was not entirely peaceful; Novgorod had to be baptized 'by fire' according to later chronicles.

Provides a summary of Vladimir's baptism, emphasizing his political calculations: he sought to strengthen his rule and gain a Byzantine bride. The peaceful aspect is highlighted via the choice of faith story (envoys' reports), but also notes that the baptism was imposed, and resistance occurred in some regions. The primary reason was to unify the realm and increase prestige.

Discusses Vladimir's conversion, focusing on the Primary Chronicle's account of his envoys' report of Hagia Sophia's beauty. It notes that the adoption was a state act, with mass baptisms in Kiev being relatively peaceful (people entering the Dnieper), but also mentions the coercion in Novgorod. Reasons: religious aesthetics, political ties with Byzantium, and consolidation of power.

Orthodox perspective: baptism was a gradual process initiated by Vladimir after his own conversion at Chersonesos. It describes the mass baptism in Kiev as peaceful and voluntary, though acknowledges that in other cities, such as Novgorod, the process was accompanied by violence and force. Reasons: Vladimir's personal faith, the desire to unite the nation under one God, and the need for a state church.

Details Vladimir's personal conversion story: he accepted Christianity after considering various faiths, married Anna Porphyrogenita, and then baptized his people. It mentions that the conversion was largely peaceful in Kiev but faced resistance elsewhere. The reasons were geopolitical (alliance with Byzantium), cultural, and personal (he became a devout Christian).

Travel/history site summarizes: Vladimir's baptism in 988 was a turning point. It describes the mass baptism in Dnieper as a peaceful event with many people coming voluntarily, but notes that Novgorod resisted and was forcibly baptized. Reasons: Vladimir's search for a unifying religion that would strengthen the state and his marriage to Anna.

News article recap: The baptism was a state-driven process. It describes the peaceful mass baptism in Kiev, but also the 'baptism by fire' in Novgorod where Dobrynya and Putyata used force. Reasons: political alliance with Byzantium, the need for a state religion, and Vladimir's personal choice after being impressed by Byzantine Christianity.

Explores the aesthetic appeal: Vladimir's envoys were moved by the beauty of Hagia Sophia, which influenced his choice. The article mentions that the conversion was a mix of peaceful acceptance and coercive measures. It emphasizes the cultural and spiritual reasons, but also acknowledges the political motivations and the resistance in regions like Novgorod.

Educational content: Vladimir's adoption of Christianity was a calculated political move to centralize power and align with Byzantium. It states that the baptism of Kiev was relatively peaceful, with people gathered in the river, but chronicles record violence in Novgorod. Reasons: economic ties, cultural prestige, and dynastic marriage.

Similar to the Baptism of Rus' article: describes the gradual Christianization, with Vladimir's baptism at Chersonesos. It notes that in Kiev the event was peaceful, but in Novgorod there was a pagan rebellion that was suppressed. Reasons: Vladimir's personal conversion, political advantages, and the role of his grandmother Olga.

This shift indicates a reason: the failure of pagan reform to unify the state, leading to a peaceful adoption of Christianity as a superior alternative.

Overview of Slavic paganism: Vladimir's pagan reform in 980 was an attempt to create a unified pantheon. When it failed to provide the desired centralization, he turned to Christianity. This suggests that the conversion was a peaceful political decision after a failed experiment, rather than a forced imposition from the start.

Khors was a solar deity in the pantheon. Vladimir's reform included Khors. The failure of this reform is cited as a reason for adopting Christianity. The switch was a top-down decision, peaceful in terms of royal policy, though enforcement later faced resistance.

Blog post on Slavic paganism: describes Vladimir's early pagan reform. It notes that after this reform failed to strengthen the state, he considered other religions. His choice of Christianity was based on political and cultural reasons, and the initial acceptance in Kiev was peaceful.

context_sources.description: Wikipedia article details the Christianization of Kievan Rus', stating that Vladimir the Great officially adopted Christianity in 988. Reasons include Vladimir's desire for political legitimacy, marriage alliance with Byzantine emperor Basil II, and the influence of Byzantine culture. The article notes that the conversion was not entirely peaceful; Novgorod had to be baptized 'by fire' according to later chronicles.

Explores the aesthetic appeal: Vladimir's envoys were moved by the beauty of Hagia Sophia, which influenced his choice. It emphasizes the cultural and spiritual reasons, but also acknowledges the political motivations and the resistance in regions like Novgorod.

Educational content: Vladimir's adoption of Christianity was a calculated political move to centralize power and align with Byzantium. It states that the baptism of Kiev was relatively peaceful, with people gathered in the river, but chronicles record violence in Novgorod. Reasons: economic ties, cultural prestige, and dynastic marriage.

Similar to the Baptism of Rus' article: describes the gradual Christianization, with Vladimir's baptism at Chersonesos. It notes that in Kiev the event was peaceful, but in Novgorod there was a pagan rebellion that was suppressed. Reasons: Vladimir's personal conversion, political advantages, and the role of his grandmother Olga.

Orthodox perspective: baptism was a gradual process initiated by Vladimir after his own conversion at Chersonesos. Reasons: Vladimir's personal faith, the desire to unite the nation under one God, and the need for a state church.

research_question: На агрегате непрерывного горячего цинкования (АНГЦ) стальная полоса сначала проходит через печь термохимического отжига, где в контролируемой восстановительной атмосфере (обычно смесь водорода и азота) происходит удаление оксидов, рекристаллизация структуры и формирование требуемых механических свойств, после чего очищенная и активированная поверхность направляется в ванну с расплавленным цинком, где за счёт диффузионного взаимодействия железа и цинка формируется многослойное покрытие; далее избыточный металл снимается газовыми ножами, обеспечивая заданную толщину покрытия. Одним из характерных дефектов является «отслой» — нарушение адгезии цинкового покрытия к стали, проявляющееся в виде отслаивания или шелушения слоя. Какие проблемы могут приводить к появлению отслоя, и какими действиями можно их устранить?

context_sources.description: Eurotherm application note on continuous annealing and galvanizing lines. Describes furnace temperature control, dew point measurement, and atmosphere composition (H2/N2) to ensure proper reduction of oxides. Improper dew point (> -40°C) leads to incomplete reduction and subsequent poor coating adhesion, contributing to delamination. Recommends tight control of H2 concentration (5-10%) and dew point monitoring.

GalvanizeIT overview of continuous sheet galvanizing. Explains the process: cleaning, annealing in reducing atmosphere, dipping in Zn bath. Mentions that surface oxides on steel (especially Si, Mn) can inhibit Fe-Zn reaction and cause peeling. Emphasizes the need for proper pre-treatment and bath Al control to form Fe2Al5 inhibition layer for adhesion.

Similar to the US version; covers temperature control in radiant tube and direct-fired furnaces. Highlights that non-uniform temperature or incorrect dew point can cause local oxidation, leading to bare spots and delamination. Recommends cascade control and oxygen probes.

Linde brochure on industrial gases for heat treatment. Discusses H2/N2 atmospheres for annealing. States that hydrogen reduces iron oxides; insufficient H2 or high dew point results in residual oxides that prevent wetting by zinc, causing coating separation. Suggests 5-20% H2 and dew point below -40°C for clean surfaces.

Patent US9713823B2 on a method for continuous galvanizing. Describes controlling the atmosphere in the snout to prevent reoxidation. Reoxidation of the strip before entry into the bath leads to Fe-Zn intermetallic growth inhibition failure and delamination. Proposes using a sealed snout with reducing gas and controlled oxygen level.

Figure showing annealing cycle in a galvanizing simulator. Illustrates heating, soaking, and cooling stages. Indicates that too rapid cooling or insufficient soaking time can leave unreduced oxides, which act as weak interfaces and cause flaking. Optimal cycle depends on steel grade.

Andritz description of galvanizing line furnaces. Mentions direct-fired furnace (DFF) and radiant tube furnace (RTF). DFF can cause selective oxidation of Si and Mn on AHSS, leading to poor coatability and delamination. Suggests using pre-oxidation or controlled cooling to mitigate.

ScienceDirect article on the effect of dew point on coating adhesion. Abstract states that increasing dew point from -30°C to -10°C promotes internal oxidation of Si/Mn, which reduces the formation of the Fe2Al5 inhibition layer. This results in brittle intermetallic phases and subsequent peeling. Optimal dew point < -40°C for AHSS.

Eurotherm industry page; covers control strategies for annealing furnaces. Emphasizes that precise control of reducing gas composition and temperature uniformity prevents oxide formation. Mentions that poor control leads to 'skipping' or 'uncoated areas' which are precursors to delamination.

GalvInfoNote 2015 presentation. Summarizes common defects: dross, bare spots, flaking. Flaking (peeling) is attributed to excessive intermetallic growth due to high bath temperature (>465°C) or low Al content. Recommendations: keep Al 0.13-0.18%, temperature 450-460°C, and control immersion time.

context_sources.description: GalvInfoNote 2-4-1 Rev 2 on aluminum in the zinc bath. Explains that Al is essential to form a thin Fe2Al5 inhibition layer that ensures coating adhesion. If Al is too low (<0.10%), thick Fe-Zn intermetallics form, which are brittle and flake off. If too high (>0.18%), excessive dross forms. Recommended range 0.13-0.16%.

Article on the effect of Al on Fe2Al5 formation. Concludes that the inhibition layer is critical for adhesion; its absence leads to uncontrolled Fe-Zn reactions and powdery, non-adherent coating. Mentions that strip entry temperature influences the reaction kinetics.

GalvInfoNote 2.4.1 (same as above but slightly different) – details on bath Al control. Advises that Al concentration must be maintained within tight limits; deviation causes either thick intermetallic (delamination) or dross-related surface defects. Also discusses effect of steel Si content.

ACS Omega article on reoxidation in the snout. Reports that oxygen ingress into the snout causes reoxidation of the strip surface, destroying the clean Fe surface needed for wetting. Reoxidation leads to poor adhesion and flaking. Solutions include inert gas purging and sealing.

Study on heat transfer and dross formation. Links high strip entry temperature (>480°C) to increased dross formation (top dross) which can be entrapped in the coating, causing local weak spots and later peeling. Recommends entry temperature 460-470°C.

GalvInfoNote 2.4 on the role of Al in galvanizing. States that Al promotes formation of a compact Fe2Al5 layer; without it, the coating consists of delta and zeta phases that are thick and brittle. This brittle layer easily cracks and delaminates under bending.

Article on advanced high strength steels (AHSS). Describes that Si and Mn in AHSS are prone to selective oxidation during annealing, resulting in a surface oxide film that hinders wetting. This oxide film causes 'bare spots' and delamination. Pre-oxidation or increased H2 can reduce this.

OSTI report on galvanizing of high-Si steels. Confirms the Sandelin effect: Si content >0.1% causes excessive Fe-Zn reaction, leading to thick, brittle coating that flakes. Remedies include adding Ni or adjusting bath Al and temperature.

GalvInfoNote 2022 update. Compiles causes of adhesion failure: (1) insufficient cleaning, (2) high dew point, (3) low Al in bath, (4) high strip entry temperature, (5) dross entrapment. Provides action table: reduce dew point, increase H2, optimize Al, lower temperature, improve snout sealing.

Article on inhibition layer growth. Finds that immersion time (2-5 seconds) is critical; too short prevents full layer formation, too long causes excessive growth and brittleness. Optimal time depends on bath temperature and steel grade.

Какие проблемы могут приводить к появлению отслоя, и какими действиями можно их устранить?

context_sources.description: Patent application for a direct-fired burner unit. Discusses control of combustion to avoid oxidizing atmosphere. Excess oxygen in DFF leads to surface oxidation of the strip, especially Si and Mn, which then reduces galvanizing adhesion. Proposes oxygen sensors.

Andritz description of galvanizing line furnaces. Mentions direct-fired furnace (DFF) and radiant tube furnace (RTF). DFF can cause selective oxidation of Si and Mn on AHSS, leading to poor coatability and delamination. Suggests using pre-oxidation or controlled cooling to mitigate.

European patent on pre-oxidation of AHSS. Describes a pre-oxidation step before annealing to control surface segregation of Si and Mn. This reduces the amount of surface oxides that cause wetting problems, thus preventing delamination.

Similar to earlier Andritz page; further details on furnace types. Notes that DFF is suitable for low-alloy steels but problematic for AHSS due to selective oxidation. Recommends use of RTF or pre-oxidation for high-Si/Mn grades.

News article about a galvanizing line installation. Mentions that the line includes advanced snout atmosphere control to prevent reoxidation – a key measure to avoid delamination. Reports improved coating adhesion after installation.

AHSS Insights reference on galvanizing. Summarizes that surface oxides from Si and Mn are the main cause of poor adhesion in AHSS. Solutions: pre-oxidation, higher H2 in annealing, and addition of Ni to the bath.

ResearchGate article on industrial pre-oxidation. Reports that pre-oxidation at 400-600°C in air creates a controlled oxide layer that suppresses internal oxidation of Si/Mn during annealing, thereby improving zinc wetting and eliminating delamination.

SMS group description of galvanizing lines. Emphasizes the importance of the cooling section and snout atmosphere. Poor atmosphere control leads to reoxidation and flaking. Suggests using a closed snout with N2-H2 purge.

Scribd document (presentation) on galvanizing AHSS. Lists causes of delamination: high surface oxides, insufficient reduction, high strip entry temperature, low bath Al. Actions: adjust annealing cycle, control dew point, add Al, reduce entry temperature.

Cambridge article on optimizing Mn and Si for galvanizing. Concludes that higher Si and Mn cause selective oxidation and poor coatability. Recommended limits: Si<0.3%, Mn<2% for good adhesion, or use of special pre-treatments.

context_sources.description: Matest Labs summary of ASTM A653. Standard specifies adhesion testing by bend tests. Delamination is evaluated by bending and observing flaking. Provides acceptance criteria for coating adherence.

ASTM store page for A653/A653M. Describes the standard for hot-dip galvanized steel sheet. Includes requirements for coating adhesion, which must withstand bending without peeling. Failure indicates poor process conditions.
ASTM official page for A653. Notes that adhesion is verified by bend tests; the coating should not flake or peel. Provides guidance on sample preparation and interpretation of results.

BuildSite summary of ASTM A653. Mentions that the standard covers steel sheet coated with zinc or zinc-iron alloy. Adhesion is critical; defects such as flaking are unacceptable and indicate issues like poor cleaning or improper bath chemistry.

Redline version of ASTM A653. Details the bend test procedure for adhesion. A 180° bend is made; if the coating separates (peels) from the base metal, it is considered a failure. This directly relates to delamination defects.

Comparison of ASTM A653 and Chinese standard. Highlights that both require adhesion bend tests. Notes that common reasons for failing the bend test include excessive intermetallic layers (due to low Al) and surface contamination.

ICCSafe shop for ASTM A653. Reiterates that the standard is used to evaluate coating quality; poor adhesion leads to rejection. Suggests that controlling furnace atmosphere and bath composition is essential to pass the test.

Alro Steel product page for galvanized steel. Mentions that the material conforms to ASTM A653; coating adhesion is a key quality attribute. Problems like flaking can arise from manufacturing deviations.

CosaSteel summary of A653. Notes that the standard includes requirements for coating weight and adhesion. Delamination is often linked to improper cleaning or bath Al content. Recommends regular process checks.

Vitina Roof blog on ASTM A653. Explains that the bend test ensures the coating does not peel. Common causes of failure: zinc bath temperature too high, insufficient Al, and contaminated strip surface.

context_sources.description: ResearchGate article on strip temperature and dross. Finds that entry temperature above 475°C increases dross formation, which can be trapped in the coating. Dross particles act as stress risers, promoting local delamination during forming.

GalvInfoNote 2-4-1 Rev 2 on aluminum in the zinc bath. Explains that Al is essential to form a thin Fe2Al5 inhibition layer that ensures coating adhesion. If Al is too low (<0.10%), thick Fe-Zn intermetallics form, which are brittle and flake off. If too high (>0.18%), excessive dross forms. Recommended range 0.13-0.16%.

Numerical simulation study. Concludes that optimal strip entry temperature is 455-465°C. Higher temperatures accelerate Fe dissolution and dross formation, leading to coating defects including poor adhesion and peeling.

GalvInfoNote 2.4.1 (same as above but slightly different) – details on bath Al control. Advises that Al concentration must be maintained within tight limits; deviation causes either thick intermetallic (delamination) or dross-related surface defects. Also discusses effect of steel Si content.

Article on zinc dross. Dross particles (top and bottom) can become embedded in the coating, causing weak spots that flake off. Dross formation is controlled by bath temperature, Al content, and strip entry temperature.

IOP conference paper on dross. Reports that top dross (Fe2Al5) and bottom dross (FeZn13) both affect coating quality. Bottom dross particles cause surface roughness and adhesion loss. Control of Al and temperature minimizes dross.

Wikipedia overview of hot-dip galvanizing. Mentions that the process involves cleaning, fluxing, and dipping. Defects include bare spots, dross, and peeling. Peeling is often caused by insufficient cleaning, high Si content, or improper bath composition.

Finishing.com discussion on galvanizing defects. Operators report delamination issues linked to high dew point in the annealing furnace. Advice: lower dew point to -30°C or below, increase H2, and ensure proper snout sealing.

Study on dross improvement at CSC. Found that reducing strip entry temperature and optimizing Al reduced dross and improved coating adhesion. Also highlighted the role of skimming and bath maintenance.

PMC article on galvanizing defects (open access). Discusses the mechanism of peeling: mainly due to formation of thick ζ-phase (FeZn13) when the inhibition layer fails. Failure occurs due to low Al or high bath temperature. Remedies: adjust Al to 0.13-0.15%, maintain 450°C.

Research question:
На агрегате непрерывного горячего цинкования (АНГЦ) стальная полоса сначала проходит через печь термохимического отжига, где в контролируемой восстановительной атмосфере (обычно смесь водорода и азота) происходит удаление оксидов, рекристаллизация структуры и формирование требуемых механических свойств, после чего очищенная и активированная поверхность направляется в ванну с расплавленным цинком, где за счёт диффузионного взаимодействия железа и цинка формируется многослойное покрытие; далее избыточный металл снимается газовыми ножами, обеспечивая заданную толщину покрытия. context_sources.description: GalvInfoNote 2.7 on snout atmosphere. Explains that the snout must have a reducing atmosphere to prevent reoxidation of the strip after annealing. Oxygen ingress leads to formation of ZnO and FeO, which prevent wetting and cause delamination. Use N2-H2 purge with <50 ppm O2.

ACS Omega article on reoxidation in the snout. Reports that oxygen ingress into the snout causes reoxidation of the strip surface, destroying the clean Fe surface needed for wetting. Reoxidation leads to poor adhesion and flaking. Solutions include inert gas purging and sealing.

Patent US4557953 on galvanizing snout design. Describes a snout with a gas seal to prevent air infiltration. The invention aims to reduce reoxidation and improve coating adhesion, thus eliminating peeling defects.

GalvanizeIT publication on zinc coatings. Covers the formation of intermetallic layers. States that the Fe2Al5 inhibition layer is crucial for adhesion; its absence leads to uncontrolled growth of brittle intermetallics that spall. Emphasizes Al control.

Drever company page on galvanizing snouts. Offers snout atmosphere control systems. Notes that proper atmosphere prevents reoxidation, which is a major cause of coating peeling. Systems include dew point and oxygen analyzers.

ISIJ International article on snout oxidation. Finds that reoxidation of the strip in the snout is directly correlated with peel defects. The extent of reoxidation depends on oxygen partial pressure and temperature. Recommends using high-purity N2.

GalvanizeIT overview of continuous sheet galvanizing. Explains the process: cleaning, annealing in reducing atmosphere, dipping in Zn bath. Mentions that surface oxides on steel (especially Si, Mn) can inhibit Fe-Zn reaction and cause peeling. Emphasizes the need for proper pre-treatment and bath Al control to form Fe2Al5 inhibition layer for adhesion.

Finishing.com thread on flaking. Users identify that flaking occurs when the coating is too thick or contains excessive dross. Solutions: adjust air knives, control bath temperature, and maintain Al level.

Article on the effect of snout atmosphere. Concludes that oxygen in the snout causes surface oxidation of Fe, resulting in poor wetting. The oxide layer acts as a barrier, leading to non-adherent coating. Use of reducing gas mixtures prevents this.

Wikipedia overview of hot-dip galvanizing. Mentions that the process involves cleaning, fluxing, and dipping. Defects include bare spots, dross, and peeling. Peeling is often caused by insufficient cleaning, high Si content, or improper bath composition.

context_sources.description: GalvInfoNote 2-4-1 Rev 2 on aluminum in the zinc bath. Explains that Al is essential to form a thin Fe2Al5 inhibition layer that ensures coating adhesion. If Al is too low (<0.10%), thick Fe-Zn intermetallics form, which are brittle and flake off. If too high (>0.18%), excessive dross forms. Recommended range 0.13-0.16%.

GalvInfoNote 2022 update. Compiles causes of adhesion failure: (1) insufficient cleaning, (2) high dew point, (3) low Al in bath, (4) high strip entry temperature, (5) dross entrapment. Provides action table: reduce dew point, increase H2, optimize Al, lower temperature, improve snout sealing.

GalvInfoNote 2015 presentation. Summarizes common defects: dross, bare spots, flaking. Flaking (peeling) is attributed to excessive intermetallic growth due to high bath temperature (>465°C) or low Al content. Recommendations: keep Al 0.13-0.18%, temperature 450-460°C, and control immersion time.

GalvInfoNote 2014 presentation. Lists major defects: dross, bare spots, flaking. Flaking is caused by (a) low Al, (b) high Si in steel, (c) high bath temperature. Actions: increase Al to 0.15%, decrease temperature to 450°C, and add Ni for high-Si steels.

Study on immersion time and temperature. Finds that longer immersion (>5 sec) and higher temperature (>460°C) promote thick intermetallic layers (delta and zeta) that are brittle and prone to flaking. Optimal immersion: 2-4 sec at 450°C.

ResearchGate article on immersion time. Reports that increasing immersion time from 1 to 6 seconds increases coating thickness but also increases the proportion of brittle phases, leading to poor bend test results (delamination).

GalvInfoNote 2.3 on coating thickness. Discusses that excessively thick coatings are more susceptible to flaking. Thickness is controlled by air knives and bath temperature. Recommends thickness targets based on steel grade.

Taylor & Francis article on galvanizing defects. Provides statistical analysis linking delamination to: high bath temperature, low Al, and high strip entry temperature. Suggests real-time monitoring of these parameters.

Surface treatments guide. Emphasizes that oil residues and dirt on the strip surface prevent zinc wetting, causing bare spots that later peel. Proper alkaline degreasing and electrolytic cleaning are essential.

GalvanizeIT on HDG coating. Describes the metallurgical bond between zinc and steel. Adhesion failure occurs when the Fe-Zn reaction is uncontrolled. Key factors: surface preparation, bath chemistry, and temperature.

Какие проблемы могут приводить к появлению отслоя, и какими действиями можно их устранить?

7

Visited sources context:
[Source id: 1786]
URL: https://diecasting.zinc.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/9/2020/07/GalvInfoNote-2-4_1-Rev-2.pdf
Description: GalvInfoNote 2-4-1 Rev 2 on aluminum in the zinc bath. Explains that Al is essential to form a thin Fe2Al5 inhibition layer that ensures coating adhesion. If Al is too low (<0.10%), thick Fe-Zn intermetallics form, which are brittle and flake off. If too high (>0.18%), excessive dross forms. Recommended range 0.13-0.16%.

[Source id: 1794]
URL: https://www.galvinfo.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2022/01/Galvanizing-2022.pdf
Description: GalvInfoNote 2022 update. Compiles causes of adhesion failure: (1) insufficient cleaning, (2) high dew point, (3) low Al in bath, (4) high strip entry temperature, (5) dross entrapment. Provides action table: reduce dew point, increase H2, optimize Al, lower temperature, improve snout sealing.

[Source id: 1785]
URL: https://www.galvinfo.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2017/12/Galvanizing-2015.pdf
Description: GalvInfoNote 2015 presentation. Summarizes common defects: dross, bare spots, flaking. Flaking (peeling) is attributed to excessive intermetallic growth due to high bath temperature (>465°C) or low Al content. Recommendations: keep Al 0.13-0.18%, temperature 450-460°C, and control immersion time.

[Source id: 1830]
URL: https://www.galvinfo.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2017/05/Galvanizing-2014.pdf
Description: GalvInfoNote 2014 presentation. Lists major defects: dross, bare spots, flaking. Flaking is caused by (a) low Al, (b) high Si in steel, (c) high bath temperature. Actions: increase Al to 0.15%, decrease temperature to 450°C, and add Ni for high-Si steels.

[Source id: 1831]
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/337719921_Study_the_Effect_of_Immersion_Time_and_Zinc_Bath_Temperature_on_Coating_Thickness_and_Generated_Phases_in_Hot_Dip_Galvanizing
Description: Study on immersion time and temperature. Finds that longer immersion (>5 sec) and higher temperature (>460°C) promote thick intermetallic layers (delta and zeta) that are brittle and prone to flaking. Optimal immersion: 2-4 sec at 450°C.

[Source id: 1832]
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322813372_The_effect_of_immersion_time_to_low_carbon_steel_hardness_and_microstructure_with_hot_dip_galvanizing_coating_method
Description: ResearchGate article on immersion time. Reports that increasing immersion time from 1 to 6 seconds increases coating thickness but also increases the proportion of brittle phases, leading to poor bend test results (delamination).

[Source id: 1833]
URL: https://www.galvinfo.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2017/05/GalvInfoNote_2_3.pdf
Description: GalvInfoNote 2.3 on coating thickness. Discusses that excessively thick coatings are more susceptible to flaking. Thickness is controlled by air knives and bath temperature. Recommends thickness targets based on steel grade.

[Source id: 1834]
URL: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/23311916.2023.2220480
Description: Taylor & Francis article on galvanizing defects. Provides statistical analysis linking delamination to: high bath temperature, low Al, and high strip entry temperature. Suggests real-time monitoring of these parameters.

context_sources.description: Article on electrolytic degreasing. Explains that cathodic cleaning removes oils and oxides. Incomplete cleaning leaves a residue that inhibits the Fe-Zn reaction, resulting in poor adhesion and peeling. Recommends proper current density and time.

Blog on electrolytic alkaline cleaning. States that this step is critical for galvanizing; any remaining contamination causes coating defects such as blistering and delamination. Effective cleaning ensures a chemically active surface.

Industry description of degreasing. Notes that alkaline degreasing removes rolling oils and fines. If not done properly, the residue burns off in the furnace, causing carbon contamination and poor adhesion. Regular bath analysis is recommended.

Patent US9809884 on cleaning section. Describes a combination of alkaline and electrolytic cleaning to remove all contaminants. Emphasizes that clean strip surface is the first prerequisite for good adhesion.

Patent US5981084A on strip cleaning. Discusses the use of brush scrubbing and electrolytic cleaning to remove particles and oils. Claims that improved cleaning reduces coating defects, including peeling.

Patent US6346295 on cleaning in galvanizing lines. Details a method using hot alkaline solution and brushing. States that residual oil leads to 'skips' in coating, which are weak points for delamination.
ScienceDirect topic overview. Describes electrolytic cleaning as an efficient way to remove organic residues. In galvanizing, incomplete cleaning results in poor wetting and adhesion loss.
Patent US4035256 on cleaning. Proposes a two-stage cleaning process. Highlights that contaminants cause the zinc coating to not adhere, leading to flaking.

Какие проблемы могут приводить к появлению отслоя, и какими действиями можно их устранить?

context_sources.description: GalvInfoNote 2.4 on the role of Al in galvanizing. States that Al promotes formation of a compact Fe2Al5 layer; without it, the coating consists of delta and zeta phases that are thick and brittle. This brittle layer easily cracks and delaminates under bending.

Study on heat transfer and dross formation. Links high strip entry temperature (>480°C) to increased dross formation (top dross) which can be entrapped in the coating, causing local weak spots and later peeling. Recommends entry temperature 460-470°C.

GalvInfoNote 2-4-1 Rev 2 on aluminum in the zinc bath. Explains that Al is essential to form a thin Fe2Al5 inhibition layer that ensures coating adhesion. If Al is too low (<0.10%), thick Fe-Zn intermetallics form, which are brittle and flake off. If too high (>0.18%), excessive dross forms. Recommended range 0.13-0.16%.

ResearchGate article on Al content. Shows that Al levels between 0.13-0.16% yield optimal inhibition layer and minimal dross. Outside this range, coating exhibits poor adhesion (flaking) and dross defects.

GalvInfoNote 2022 update. Compiles causes of adhesion failure: (1) insufficient cleaning, (2) high dew point, (3) low Al in bath, (4) high strip entry temperature, (5) dross entrapment. Provides action table: reduce dew point, increase H2, optimize Al, lower temperature, improve snout sealing.

Article on inhibition layer growth. Finds that immersion time (2-5 seconds) is critical; too short prevents full layer formation, too long causes excessive growth and brittleness. Optimal time depends on bath temperature and steel grade.

Study on Fe2Al5 growth. Confirms that the inhibition layer forms epitaxially on the steel substrate. Disruption of this layer (e.g., by surface oxides) leads to brittle intermetallic growth and adhesion loss.

ARC journal article on dross. Discusses the formation of bottom dross (FeZn13) and top dross (Fe2Al5). Both types can be entrapped and cause coating defects. Control of Al and temperature minimizes dross and improves adhesion.

Article on the effect of Al on Fe2Al5 formation. Concludes that the inhibition layer is critical for adhesion; its absence leads to uncontrolled Fe-Zn reactions and powdery, non-adherent coating. Mentions that strip entry temperature influences the reaction kinetics.

Springer article on inhibition layer. Reports that the Fe2Al5 layer must be continuous; any discontinuity allows local Fe-Zn reaction, forming brittle phases that flake. Solutions: Al addition and reducing atmosphere.

context_sources.description: MDPI review on coatings for AHSS. Summarizes that selective oxidation of Si and Mn is the main barrier to adhesion. Pre-oxidation and higher H2 content are effective countermeasures.

ThyssenKrupp report on AHSS galvanizing. Describes that Si and Mn enrichment on the surface hinders wetting. They use a special pre-oxidation step to convert external oxides into internal ones, improving coatability and eliminating peeling.

AHSS Insights reference on galvanizing. Summarizes that surface oxides from Si and Mn are the main cause of poor adhesion in AHSS. Solutions: pre-oxidation, higher H2 in annealing, and addition of Ni to the bath.

Cambridge article on optimizing Mn and Si for galvanizing. Concludes that higher Si and Mn cause selective oxidation and poor coatability. Recommended limits: Si<0.3%, Mn<2% for good adhesion, or use of special pre-treatments.
AIST paper on AHSS galvanizing. Discusses the Sandelin effect and solutions: addition of Ni to the bath, or increasing Al content. Provides case studies showing reduced flaking after adjustments.
Article on selective oxidation. Concludes that Mn and Si oxides form during annealing, inhibiting the Fe-Al reaction. To mitigate, use a higher H2 fraction and lower dew point.
Metallurgical Research article. States that the Sandelin effect causes thick, brittle coating on Si-containing steels. Remedies: decrease bath temperature, increase Al, or add Sb.
Optimization study. Recommends limiting Si to <0.3% and Mn to <2% for good adhesion. For higher contents, use pre-oxidation or Ni pre-coating.
Applied Surface Science article. Investigates the surface chemistry of oxidized AHSS. Finds that MnO and SiO2 layers prevent formation of Fe2Al5. Removal requires aggressive reduction.
Surface & Coatings Technology article. Reports that a thin Ni layer on the steel before galvanizing can suppress the Sandelin effect and improve adhesion, preventing peeling.

context_sources.description: GalvInfoNote 2-4-1 Rev 2 on aluminum in the zinc bath. Explains that Al is essential to form a thin Fe2Al5 inhibition layer that ensures coating adhesion. If Al is too low (<0.10%), thick Fe-Zn intermetallics form, which are brittle and flake off. If too high (>0.18%), excessive dross forms. Recommended range 0.13-0.16%.

GalvInfoNote 2.4.1 (same as above but slightly different) – details on bath Al control. Advises that Al concentration must be maintained within tight limits; deviation causes either thick intermetallic (delamination) or dross-related surface defects. Also discusses effect of steel Si content.
Study on strip entry temperature. Shows that as entry temperature rises from 440 to 480°C, coating adhesion decreases due to increased intermetallic growth. Optimal entry temperature 455°C.

Study on immersion time and temperature. Finds that longer immersion (>5 sec) and higher temperature (>460°C) promote thick intermetallic layers (delta and zeta) that are brittle and prone to flaking. Optimal immersion: 2-4 sec at 450°C.

Wikipedia overview of hot-dip galvanizing. Mentions that the process involves cleaning, fluxing, and dipping. Defects include bare spots, dross, and peeling. Peeling is often caused by insufficient cleaning, high Si content, or improper bath composition.
Wuppermann process description. Explains their 'heat-to-coat' process which precisely controls the strip temperature before dipping. They claim this reduces defects like flaking.
AZZ article comparing galvanizing and plating. Notes that galvanizing adhesion is metallurgical; if the bath is too hot, brittle intermetallics form and flake. Temperature control is vital.

ACS Omega article on reoxidation in the snout. Reports that oxygen ingress into the snout causes reoxidation of the strip surface, destroying the clean Fe surface needed for wetting. Reoxidation leads to poor adhesion and flaking. Solutions include inert gas purging and sealing.

Article on the effect of bath temperature. Concludes that increasing bath temperature from 450 to 470°C accelerates Fe-Zn reaction, producing thicker and more brittle layers that are prone to spalling.

Journal of Coating Science and Technology. Discusses dross and adhesion. Bottom dross inclusions create stress concentration points, leading to localized delamination. Control of temperature and Al reduces dross.

Research question:
На агрегате непрерывного горячего цинкования (АНГЦ) стальная полоса сначала проходит через печь термохимического отжига, где в контролируемой восстановительной атмосфере (обычно смесь водорода и азота) происходит удаление оксидов, рекристаллизация структуры и формирование требуемых механических свойств, после чего очищенная и активированная поверхность направляется в ванну с расплавленным цинком, где за счёт диффузионного взаимодействия железа и цинка формируется многослойное покрытие; далее избыточный металл снимается газовыми ножами, обеспечивая заданную толщину покрытия. Одним из характерных дефектов является «отслой» — нарушение адгезии цинкового покрытия к стали, проявляющееся в виде отслаивания или шелушения слоя. Какие проблемы могут приводить к появлению отслоя, и какими действиями можно их устранить?
